blocks.content: She texted first, before the sparkling cider, before the fireplace.
There was a family group — her parents, Maya, Lena, Nathan on the edge of it now — and she opened it and typed.
We're here. The cabin is perfect. We found the card.

She set the phone on the counter. Ethan was already working on the fireplace — he'd found the matches in a small clay pot on the mantle, exactly where you'd put them if you'd put them there forty years ago and never changed the habit.
The phone lit up.
Good. Enjoy every minute. Love you both.
Then, after a brief pause, another.
Don't rush back. The mountain will do the rest.
And then Maya, because Maya.
Please tell me you found the sparkling cider. Dad spent forty-five minutes choosing it.
We found the sparkling cider.
Good. Don't text us again until you come back.
I mean it.
I love you. Don't text me.
Cassandra laughed. The kind that came out before she'd decided to laugh.
Behind her, the fireplace caught.

The first sound of it — the low, even breath of something taking hold — and the room changed. Not dramatically. Just the way a room changes when it becomes warmer, more itself. The light shifted. The shadows found their edges.
Ethan sat back on his heels and watched it a moment.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Maya says not to text until we're back.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Sound advice.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: She also said she loves me in the same text.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Also sound.
She put the phone face-down on the counter. Left it there.
